summary.leadingMessage: Renewal weakness is now cross-functional and should be closed as one board-visible save and exit-readiness workstream.

items.boardQuestion: Which AI-governance renewals are most likely to slip into exit without immediate intervention?
items.currentPosture: Renewal intent is still present, but proof of operational impact has aged.
items.renewalNarrative: The AI governance lane is sticky enough to renew if we tighten the operator proof set.
items.exitRiskReality: Rollback, exception, and eval proof is older than the renewal story implies.
items.riskHeadline: AI-governance renewals need fresher operating proof before the next board-ready commercial review.
items.renewalSignal: Pipeline stays warm, but customer confidence is now asking for newer evidence of control durability.
items.missingProof: Fresh exception-handling export, renewed eval packet, and updated rollback outcomes
items.headline: AI governance renewals are recoverable, but only if proof freshness improves fast.
items.narrative: This is a delivery-proof and board-story problem more than a category-demand problem.
items.nextMove: Refresh the operating packet and narrow the renewal story to the highest-confidence outcomes.

renewalConfidenceAssessment.message: Renewal confidence exists, but the narrative should narrow before the next cycle.

deliveryFreshnessAssessment.message: Delivery proof is aging and should be refreshed before the next commercial review.

sponsorContinuityAssessment.message: Sponsorship exists, but continuity still depends on active intervention.

churnSignalAssessment.message: A churn signal is forming and should be closed before the next review cycle.

exposureAssessment.message: Contract exposure is too high for the current renewal weakness.

items.boardQuestion: Which renewals are strongest enough to treat as commercial anchors?
items.currentPosture: Identity proof is stable and current enough to support confident renewal language.
items.renewalNarrative: Identity reviews, admin posture, and mobile protection remain renewal anchors.
items.exitRiskReality: Only one guest-access packet is aging, and sponsor continuity is still healthy.
items.riskHeadline: Identity remains the healthiest renewal cluster with only small freshness cleanup left.
items.renewalSignal: Customer and board confidence remain aligned with the underlying proof set.
remediationMoves: lock identity as reference renewal lane
items.headline: Identity renewals can close quickly with a small evidence refresh.
items.narrative: This is the benchmark lane for retention-safe proof and sponsor continuity.
items.nextMove: Refresh the guest-access export and use identity as the commercial reference lane.

renewalConfidenceAssessment.message: Renewal confidence is strong enough for the next commercial review.

deliveryFreshnessAssessment.message: Delivery proof is aging and should be refreshed before the next commercial review.

sponsorContinuityAssessment.message: Executive sponsorship is stable enough to defend the account.

churnSignalAssessment.message: No material churn signal is visible in the current account posture.

exposureAssessment.message: Contract exposure is rising faster than the account proof set.

items.boardQuestion: Where are delivery and recovery proof gaps most likely to trigger commercial hesitation?
items.currentPosture: Commercial belief in the platform is ahead of the recovery proof chain.
items.renewalNarrative: Hosting, release safety, and recovery operations are strong enough to defend renewal.
items.exitRiskReality: Backup, rollback, and recovery packets are still fragmented across too many surfaces.
items.riskHeadline: Platform renewals need escalation because the continuity proof chain is still fragmented.
items.renewalSignal: Sponsors want to renew, but board-visible continuity proof is not yet stitched together well enough.
items.missingProof: One end-to-end packet connecting hosting, rollback, backup, and recovery proof
items.headline: Platform renewals are winnable, but the continuity packet needs one owner fast.
items.narrative: The issue is evidence stitching and sponsor confidence, not lack of technical surface area.
items.nextMove: Escalate one owner to assemble the continuity packet before the next renewal checkpoint.

renewalConfidenceAssessment.message: Renewal confidence is weaker than the current renewal story implies.

deliveryFreshnessAssessment.message: Delivery proof is aging and should be refreshed before the next commercial review.

sponsorContinuityAssessment.message: Sponsorship continuity is too weak to keep the renewal path stable.

churnSignalAssessment.message: Churn signal load is already visible enough to threaten the renewal.

exposureAssessment.message: Contract exposure is too high for the current renewal weakness.

items.boardQuestion: Which renewal lane can anchor the rest of the commercial proof stack?
items.currentPosture: Procurement remains the strongest renewal and proof-reuse lane.
items.renewalNarrative: Security questionnaires, trust-center proof, and answer-pack reuse remain renewal-ready.
items.exitRiskReality: Coverage is strong, with only minor proof-index hygiene still helpful.
items.riskHeadline: Procurement is structurally strong enough to anchor the broader renewal stack.
items.renewalSignal: Renewal momentum is backed by clear proof reuse and stable commercial trust.
items.missingProof: Published proof-coverage index across the procurement stack
items.headline: Procurement should anchor the renewal proof stack.
items.narrative: Use this lane as the commercial standard for retention-safe proof reuse.
items.nextMove: Publish the proof-coverage index and lock this lane as the reference renewal surface.

items.boardQuestion: Which renewals are too early or too weak to defend without scope changes or intentional exits?
items.currentPosture: The control-plane story is promising, but price pressure is rising faster than proof depth.
items.renewalNarrative: Reporting, attribution, and release controls justify a clean commercial renewal at current pricing.
items.exitRiskReality: Cross-surface proof is too thin for the strength of the current commercial ask.
items.riskHeadline: Pricing pressure and thin proof make this renewal cluster the closest to intentional exit.
items.renewalSignal: Commercial pressure is high and the story still outruns the evidence bundle.
items.missingProof: One end-to-end packet linking reporting, release controls, and attributable outcomes
items.headline: Some pricing-sensitive renewals should be reframed as exit-managed rather than fully defended.
items.narrative: The commercial story is still valuable, but not strong enough at current pricing and proof depth.
items.nextMove: Model an exit-safe path while rebuilding the linked revenue proof packet.

items.boardQuestion: Where is sponsor fragility most likely to turn into avoidable renewal loss?
items.currentPosture: The regulated surface is compelling, but sponsor continuity and commercial reinforcement are fragile.
items.renewalNarrative: GxP, specimen, CAPA, and safety surfaces already justify a stable renewal motion.
items.exitRiskReality: Evidence is credible, but sponsor continuity and freshness are weaker than the commercial confidence suggests.
items.riskHeadline: Sponsor fragility is the main threat to this regulated renewal cluster.
items.renewalSignal: Customers still value the surface, but sponsor turnover and older proof are slowing the save case.
items.missingProof: Fresh provenance notes, updated sponsor map, and one repeatable regulated packet
items.headline: Regulated renewals need sponsorship repair before the next cycle.
items.narrative: The product surface is real, but the account defense needs fresher proof and stronger sponsor continuity.
items.nextMove: Refresh sponsor mapping and publish one repeatable regulated packet before the next renewal checkpoint.

verification: Synthetic renewal data only - no live contracts, customers, pricing schedules, or commercial account records are included.
Scores are modeled to show how Kinetic Gain can compare renewal confidence, delivery freshness, sponsor continuity, churn signals, and contract exposure in one board-readable commercial surface.
All routes are read-only and demonstrate renewal and exit-readiness monitoring, not production legal, financial, or commercial advice.
